FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, baseline 2016-2018, ages 9-10 years, 10,317 participants from 21 U.S. recruitment sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using logistic regression to estimate associations between ACEs (baseline/1-year/2-year) and cyberbullying victimization (Year 2)

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Journal of Adolescence, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper measures cyberbullying victimization (DV) vs. ACEs (IV). Cyberbullying assessed as single item: 'Have you ever been cyberbullied...online, in texts, or on social media'. No separately-measured smartphone or social media use variable paired with an individual-trait outcome. Per criteria: 'Cyberbullying alone (without a separately-measured smartphone or SM variable) does NOT qualify.'

notes: Paper examines cycle of victimization (ACEs→cyberbullying). Although 'screen time' appears in keywords, it is not analyzed as the focal SM/phone variable; cyberbullying is measured as a behavioral outcome without isolation of phone/SM as IV or DV.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 10-14 years, 9,443 participants

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using modified Poisson regression to examine associations between cyberbullying victimization/perpetration and sleep disturbance outcomes

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper in Academic Pediatrics, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: Paper examines cyberbullying (IV/DV) vs. sleep disturbance (outcome). Cyberbullying assessed via validated questionnaire: 'Have you ever been cyberbullied...online, in texts...or on social media'. No separately-measured smartphone or social media use variable as focal IV or DV. Screen time (7.26±7.59 hrs/day) is included as a covariate/confounder in adjusted models, not as the focal SM/phone variable paired with an individual-trait outcome. Per criteria: 'Cyberbullying alone (without a separately-measured smartphone or SM variable) does NOT qualify.'

notes: Paper is cross-sectional (Year 2 only). Focal relationship is cyberbullying↔sleep, not SM/phone↔individual-trait. Screen time controlled in models but not focal exposure.

FT_IC1.evidence: U.S. ABCD Study, Year 2 (2018-2020), ages 10-14 years, 10,339 participants from 21 U.S. sites

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using multiple linear regression to examine associations between sexual orientation and recreational screen time and problematic screen use

FT_IC6.evidence: Published peer-reviewed paper (preprint) in Annals of Epidemiology, 2023

FT_IC2.evidence: IV is sexual orientation (demographic: 'Are you gay or bisexual?'—yes/maybe/no). DVs are screen time and problematic use of social media, video games, mobile phones. The focal relationship is demographic-only (sexual orientation) paired with screen/SM variables as outcomes. Per criteria: 'The OTHER SIDE of the SM relationship must be an individual-trait-level variable (neural, behavioral, health, cognitive, well-being, personality, substance use, sleep, academic, etc.). NOT demographic-only.' Screen time/problematic SM use as DV with demographic IV does not meet FT-IC2 structure: SM must pair with individual-trait-level outcome (e.g., depression, sleep, academic achievement), not demographic identity.

notes: Paper reports sexual minority (vs. heterosexual) associated with 3.72 more hours daily screen time, higher problematic use of social media (0.46 higher score), video games (0.49), mobile phones (0.56). However, the 'other side' is demographic (sexual orientation), not an individual-trait outcome like depression, anxiety, sleep, academic performance, etc.
